items.q: What is the denary value of binary 10110?
items.why: Students forget to apply place values. Each position is a power of 2 from right (1, 2, 4, 8, 16...). Reading binary as denary is the most common error.

items.q: What does in-order traversal of a binary search tree produce?
items.misconception: Nodes in the order they were inserted.
items.why: Students forget the BST property: left subtree less than node less than right subtree. In-order (left, node, right) therefore visits values in sorted order - regardless of insertion order.

items.q: What is the difference between an IP address and a MAC address?
items.correct: MAC is a hardware identifier (fixed); IP is a logical network address (assignable).
items.misconception: They are the same thing with different names.
items.random_wrong: IP is for input, MAC is for output.
items.why: Students treat these as synonyms. MAC is burned into the network card (Layer 2); IP is assigned by the network (Layer 3). Both are needed to route packets to a device.
The answer examiners want is: ALU. “Control unit” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
It's Hertz. “Bits” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: How many stages are in the basic fetch-decode-execute cycle?
items.why: The real figure is 3 stages. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
